However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. {{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. qq, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. qq, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. qq, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.

== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. == Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. {{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. qq, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

(It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. qq, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{皇室祭祀}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).
{{citation needed|しようこんさい}} [[Category:ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}]] [[Category:ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}]] [[Category:行法]] [[Category:呪術]] [[Category:宗教の歴史]]

It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).
{{citation needed|しようこんさい}} [[Category:ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}]] [[Category:ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}]] [[Category:行法]] [[Category:呪術]] [[Category:宗教の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Imperial Rituals}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Shingi-kan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihon Shoki|ja|釈日本紀}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|ja|楚辞}} by {{ill|Song Yu|ja|宋玉}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|ja|屈原}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).
{{citation needed|しようこんさい}}
