<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24896185]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}} * {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}} * {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} * {{ill|Goshōrei|ja|御招霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshōrei|1=Goshōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11077320}}
== References == * Iwanami Shoten, 『日本史辞典』 (CD-ROM version, 2000). * {{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu}}, 『万葉集辞典』 (文会堂書店, January 1919). (Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noguchi Tetsurō|1=Tetsuro Noguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229699}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakade Shōshin|1=Yoshinobu Sakade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425011}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).
''''
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24896185]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. == Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.

It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.

The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakade Shōshin|1=Yoshinobu Sakade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425011}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(Included in {{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja|折口信夫全集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orikuchi Shinobu Zenshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 11, Central Kōronsha, January 1996.) * 『国史大辞典』 (吉川弘文館, 1986). * {{ill|Noguchi Tetsurō|ja|野口鉄郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noguchi Tetsurō|1=Tetsuro Noguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17229699}} et al. (eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakade Shōshin|1=Yoshinobu Sakade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425011}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.

However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|ltq=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki|1=Shaku Nihongi}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci|1=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu|1=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan|1=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki|1=Shaku Nihongi}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci|1=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu|1=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan|1=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.

(eds.), 『道教事典』 (平河出版社, 1994). * {{ill|Sakade Shōshin|ja|坂出祥伸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakade Shōshin|1=Yoshinobu Sakade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425011}} (ed.), 『「道教」の大事典』 (新人物往来社, 1994). * {{ill|Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja|渡辺勝義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabe Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, 『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:招魂祭]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}} {{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} '''Shōkon Matsuri''' (招魂祭, shōkon no matsuri) is one of the rituals and forms of exorcism performed in Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. It was also observed within the Imperial Court.
For the modern “Shōkon Matsuri” initiated for the dead at newly established {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} and {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}, see {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.
== Overview == In Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, the ritual is performed to revitalize a weakening, living person, while in Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}} it is conducted to prevent the dispersal of the soul (霊魂) of the dead. It is distinct from the {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}, which is a ritual offered in Shintō for both the living and the dead.
== Japanese Onmyōdō == From the middle of the Heian period, Onmyōdō spread among the aristocratic society. The earliest record is found in volume 8 of the "Shōki Moku-roku" (御祭事付解除) entry dated “the eleventh day of October in Eien 2” (永延二年十月十一日招魂祭時), with “Eien 2” corresponding to 988. It was believed that a human being possesses a “tamashii” (soul) that might separate from the weakened body during deep sleep or when burdened by worry. Therefore, during illness or childbirth, rituals were performed to call back the separated soul—for example, by waving garments on the roof. These rites were exclusively performed for the living; performing them for the dead was forbidden, and those who broke this taboo were punished (see the entry in the {{ill|Sakyōki|ja|左経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyōki|1=Sakeiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479706}} dated August 23, 1025). For instance, when {{ill|Fujiwara Uresuko|ja|藤原嬉子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara Uresuko|1=Fujiwara no Kishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623137}} died in August 1025, the onmyōji {{ill|Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja|中原恒盛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara Tsuneomori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} performed a Shōkon Matsuri and nearly incurred a penalty. This prohibition on performing the ritual for the dead is a major difference between Japanese {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}. (It is explained that from the early Heian period onward, as the functions of the Onmyōryō expanded and began to overlap with those of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, the taboo against onmyōji involving matters of life and death was enforced because the Shingi-kan’s handling of Shintō’s avoidance of death and impurity gradually influenced the realm of Onmyōdō. For example, even if an onmyōji divined that a person’s illness was caused by vengeful spirits or monsters, the subsequent ritual was left to Buddhist priests.) However, this strict rule applied to official (官人) onmyōji, and it is unclear whether it was equally enforced among folk onmyōji, who in some cases did exorcise monsters.<ref>張麗山「東アジアの視点から見る日本陰陽寮の病気対策」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 25–28.</ref> (Also, aside from the Shōkon Matsuri, in the later centuries onmyōji in aristocratic circles became involved in expelling vengeful spirits and monsters.<ref>赤澤春彦「日本中世における病・物気と陰陽道」 in 小山聡子 (ed.), 『前近代日本の病気治癒と呪術』 (思文閣出版, 2020) ISBN 978-4-7842-1988-9, pp. 172–173.</ref>)
== Usage and Distinction from Chinkon-sai == As a term, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} (volume 29, entry for the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu on the 24th day [丙寅]) states “For this day, it is the Emperor’s Shōkon” (是日為天皇'''招魂'''之). However, in the {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihon Shoki|1=Shaku Nihongi}} (volume 21, secret instruction 6)<ref>卜部兼方『釈日本紀』 (経済雑誌社, 1898), p.835. (This observation is based on 渡辺勝義’s 『鎮魂祭の研究』.)</ref> the term is read as “Mitama-Furis,” and in volume 15 of the same work the text is interpreted as “Now it is a Chinkon-sai” (今鎮魂祭也), thereby treating the “Shōkon” of the Nihon Shoki as referring to a {{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}. Similarly, {{ill|Tomonobu Otomo|ja|伴信友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonobu Otomo|1=Ban Nobutomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346747}} in his work 『Chinkon Den』 describes the ritual as “that which is called Shōkon, fashioned in the Chinese style”<ref>『伴信友全集』 Vol.2 (国書刊行会, 1907), pp. 653–654.</ref> and later in 『Hikobai』 Vol.20 states, “During the daytime ceremonies of Emperor Go-Daigo, the daily ritual called ‘Seukon’ was held; this ‘Seukon’ is Shōkon, and it is not a Chinkon-sai, which is performed separately by the Onmyō clan.”<ref>伴信友「比古婆衣」 Vol.20, in 『伴信友全集』 Vol.4 (国書刊行会, 1907), p.436.</ref> These observations clearly distinguish between the Shōkon Matsuri and the Chinkon-sai.<ref>渡辺勝義『鎮魂祭の研究』 (名著出版, 1994), pp. 201–209.</ref>
== Chinese Taoism == In Chinese {{ill|Taoism|en|Taoism|WD=Q9598|lt=Taoism|1=Taoism}}, Shōkon Matsuri are also performed for the dead; however, they are not rites for resurrection. For example, in the pre-imperial [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]] the “Zhaohun Pian” in the {{ill|Chu Ci|en|Chu Ci|WD=Q874601|lt=Chu Ci|1=Chu Ci}} by {{ill|Song Yu|en|Song Yu|WD=Q431510|lt=Song Yu|1=Song Yu}} was performed to commemorate the death of {{ill|Qu Yuan|en|Qu Yuan|WD=Q313330|lt=Qu Yuan|1=Qu Yuan}}, who died without sin but was pursued by treacherous men. In the “Guoshang” section of the Nine Songs in the Chu Ci, it is stated that “once the body has died, the spirit and soul become like a heroic ghost” (身既に死して神以て霊・魂魄毅として鬼雄となる). Even today, one to three years after death, the deceased’s name is inscribed on a permanent memorial tablet called a “Shinshu” (神主) (a term also used in Confucianism, analogous to Buddhist memorial tablets), and the deceased is enshrined as a new ancestor. In this way, though the deceased are in a sense ghosts, they are regarded as ancestors who protect the happiness and safety of their descendants.
